item.title: Pope Leo is celebrated as he visits Angola
item.description: Pope Leo’s Africa tour is drawing large turnouts and warm welcomes. In Angola, the mood is celebratory, while in Washington D-C, not so much, as Pope Leo addresses the US President's criticism of him.

item.title: Free RSV vaccinations for older Australians
item.description: The Federal Government has announced free R-S-V vaccinations for older Australians, in a push to better protect those most at risk from the potentially deadly respiratory virus. The $445 million dollar investment means Australians aged 75 and over - and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people aged 60 and over - will be able to access the immunisation for free from mid May, saving them as much as $300 dollars per vaccination.

item.title: A country under 'strain': Australians' life satisfaction hits record low
item.description: New data shows life satisfaction has hit a record low, dipping below levels seen during COVID-19 lockdowns.

item.title: $2 petrol prices likely here to stay, as government looks to new instant tax deduction
item.description: Energy stocks lag, as oil prices remained volatile around supply concerns in the Strait of Hormuz, even as petrol prices eased, though still well above last year’s levels. Market analyst Evan Lucas unpacks what it all means for households and investors, as attention also turns to the federal government’s new $1,000 instant tax deduction set to benefit more than six million workers. Mark Chapman from H&R Block explains who gains from that, how it works, and when the savings will flow through.

item.title: Tsunami warning issued after powerful earthquake strikes off northern Japan coast
item.description: Buildings as far away as Tokyo shook during the tremor, and authorities warned further quakes could strike in coming days.

item.title: Already under pressure, Australia’s food system could now be in big trouble
item.description: Australia should be a rich global food bowl, but the future is looking more uncertain — for a range of reasons.

item.title: NASA rolls out core stage for the next mission to the moon: Artemis III
item.description: It's been a week since the crew of Artemis II (2) dropped back to earth after a record-breaking mission, but concrete steps are already being taken to achieve the next phase of NASA's lunar goals. The Michoud Assembly Facility in New Orleans has rolled out the largest section of NASA's Space Launch System rocket, which will launch a second crewed mission known as Artemis III (3).

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 21 April 2026
item.description: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: A $71bn rebuild and 77-year setback: A 'catastrophic' crisis in the Middle East
item.description: The cost of rebuilding Gaza and the "catastrophic" human toll of war have been laid bare in a new report.

item.title: SBS World News talks to Assistant Minister for Citizenship Julian Hill
item.description: SBS federal political reporters Anna Henderson, Rayane Tamer, and Naveen Razik have an in-depth conversation with Assistant Minister for Citizenship, Customs, and Multicultural Affairs, Julian Hill, about visa categories, and the task the government faces in dealing with humanitarian visas and family reunion.

item.title: Markets pause, as Apple turns the page on Tim Cook's era
item.description: The ASX 200 edged lower as investors held back ahead of a key Middle East ceasefire deadline, with Michael Wayne from Medallion Financial Group unpacking the day’s market moves. At the same time, Apple enters a new chapter as Tim Cook steps down after 15 years, having overseen explosive growth and the launch of products like the Apple Watch and AirPods. Stephanie Youssef speaks with Wedbush Securities’ Dan Ives about Cook’s legacy, the challenges facing incoming CEO John Ternus, and why artificial intelligence could define Apple’s next phase.

item.title: Pressure builds to lift Australia's 'tiny' gas tax
item.description: As calls mount for a 25 per cent tax on gas exports, a leading economist has trashed the existing measures at a Senate inquiry.

item.title: The 'horrific' truth about the romance scammers targeting vulnerable Australians
item.description: Behind the everyday scams targeting Australians, there are scores of vulnerable victims trafficked into the job.

item.title: SBS World News talks to Immigration Minister Tony Burke
item.description: SBS News political reporter Anna Henderson speaks to Immigration Minister Tony Burke about visas, the War in the Middle East, and IS brides. Mr Burke says federal Opposition Leader Angus Taylor is trying to turn Australians against one another with his policies.

item.title: Trump remains defiant on US blockade of Iranian ports | SBS News in Easy English 22 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: 'We're the mugs here': Is Japan getting Australian gas for free?
item.description: Discussions on Australia's gas and oil resources are heating up as a 25 per cent gas export tax gains traction.

item.title: With a degenerative disability, Jane is unsure of her future. Now she's fearful of NDIS cuts
item.description: Disability advocates have flagged concerns that an overhaul of the NDIS scheme could disproportionately impact parts of the community.

item.title: 'Interconnected challenges': Why Lebanon's future could determine the course of war
item.description: Israel and Lebanon agreed to implement a "cessation of hostilities" for 10 days, but time is running out for a peace deal.

item.title: Woolworths executive says 'prices dropped' rules changed as inflation grew
item.description: The ACCC claims the discounted prices were often the same or higher than the original shelf prices.

item.title: Super funds recover March fall as middle east war impacts company earnings
item.description: The Australian sharemarket slipped 1.1 per cent, yet remains within reach of a record high, continuing its recovery from March’s downturn as super funds rebound and long-term gains hold steady. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Ricardo Gonçalves unpacks the day’s biggest moves, including Cochlear’s dramatic 40 per cent fall to a 10-year low after a profit warning tied to weak US consumer confidence and global uncertainty. Portfolio manager James Gerrish from Market Partners joins the conversation to discuss whether other companies will also be hit by the war in the Middle-East.

item.title: Iranian gunboat fires at container ship off Oman, ship off Iran also targeted
item.description: The vessel had been sailing under a Liberian flag and "had been informed it had permission to transit the Strait of Hormuz."

item.title: Concerned about footing the bill for NDIS changes, states are pushing back
item.description: In address to the National Press Club on Wednesday, Mark Butler said states would have "greater funding responsibility" for the scheme.

item.title: Online watchdog puts video game giants on notice as child grooming concerns rise
item.description: The news comes after Roblox recently introduced new account types for underage users as pressure from global regulators mounted.

item.title: ‘That future was gone’: The pain of losing your life partner young
item.description: The death of a partner can feel debilitating at any age, but in your 20s and 30s, it brings unique challenges.

item.title: 'Can no longer be stretched': The 'defining shift' that has renters at their limits
item.description: Sky-high rents persist, but price growth is slowing and diverging across Australia's capitals, according to a report.

item.title: Iran seizes two ships crossing Hormuz, says it won't reopen strait while US blockade remains
item.description: Iran's Revolutionary Guards said their naval forces had seized two container ships trying to cross the strategic strait.

item.title: Lebanese journalist Amal Khalil killed by Israeli strike amid ceasefire
item.description: Rescuers said they had recovered the body of Amal Khalil after she was killed in an Israeli airstrike.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Why have the ceasefire talks stalled?
item.description: Ceasefire talks between the US and Iran seem to be on hold. Pakistan had planned to host a second round of talks, but the White House suspended Vice President JD Vance’s planned trip to Islamabad - as Iran rebuffed efforts to restart negotiations. Pakistani officials say authorities will keep security arrangements in place in Islamabad in case US and Iranian delegations ultimately arrive. Speaking to the Associated Press, Qamar Cheema, the Executive Director of the Sanober Institute in Islamabad, says talks between the U.S. and Iran have stalled due to a lack of clarity and a complex power struggle within Tehran

item.title: Who gets a public holiday for Anzac Day across Australia?
item.description: Anzac Day falls on a Saturday this year, with new rule changes coming into effect for the first time.

item.title: Iran seizes ships in Strait of Hormuz in escalation of naval blockade
item.description: Iran has seized two ships crossing the Strait of Hormuz. It says it won't reopen the crucial waterway while a US blockade of its vessels and ports remains in place.

item.title: Ship captain talks of distress after 54 days stranded in Persian Gulf | SBS News in Easy English 23 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: James Valentine remembered as a voice of warmth and wit after his death at 64
item.description: Radio and music personality James Valentine has died two years after being diagnosed with cancer.

item.title: Who holds the power? The key question behind Trump's latest Iran demand
item.description: The US president wants a "unified" response — but some argue there could be "division" among Iranian officials on a potential peace deal.

item.title: TACO Trump? An idiot’s guide to Strait talking & Marcos Jnr gym junkie
item.description: Confusion deepens on the Strait of Hormuz as President Trump extends the ceasefire with Iran. Another truce hangs in the balance with Israel and Hezbollah trading accusations - while thousands of Israeli troops draw a line in southern Lebanon. Plus, Japan loosens its long-standing arms export restrictions and world leaders flex their fitness credentials.

item.title: Advocates cheer decision to make showers free for home-based aged care
item.description: The lion's share of attention this week has been on the National Disability Insurance Scheme, with the federal government announcing cuts that it's estimated will see tens of thousands removed from the scheme. But there have also been developments in aged care too- the federal government has now reversed its policy on the funding for showering after a mass of complaints from residents, families, and advocates.

item.title: Employers seeking more workers with AI skills, ASX pulls further away from record
item.description: The ASX slipped 0.6 per cent amid rising geopolitical tensions in the Gulf, with losses led by materials and the big banks, while energy stocks climbed on a surge in oil prices. Against that backdrop, a sharp shift in the jobs market is emerging, with SEEK reporting a 75 per cent rise in advertised roles mentioning AI skills.

item.title: Australia's Twelve Apostles draw millions. Now, their mysteries have been solved
item.description: The enigma of Australia's iconic limestone stacks has been uncovered.

item.title: Israel-Lebanon confirm three-week ceasefire extension after White House negotiations
item.description: Thursday marked the most significant test of the ceasefire yet, after Israeli strikes killed a Lebanese journalist.

item.title: UK to ban everyone born after 2008 from buying cigarettes
item.description: The British health secretary says the move will create a "smoke-free" generation.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Will things ever return to 'normal' in the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description: Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains at a virtual standstill with President Trump ordering US forces to 'shoot and kill' Iranian vessels laying mines in the Strait. The US military says it seized another tanker associated with smuggling Iranian oil in the Indian Ocean. The U.S. and Iran had declared the Strait open on Friday April 17 but Iran reimposed control on Saturday. Tehran said it was responding to a continued U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, calling it a violation of their ceasefire. So will we ever see a return to normal shipping and trade, once the strait reopens? In this episode of Weekend One on One, we hear from Alexis Ellender, senior lead analyst at maritime data firm Kpler

item.title: For Gen Z, dumb phones and CDs are the winners as they return to analog
item.description: Analog devices are making a comeback with the most tech-savvy, plugged in generation of all time - Gen Z. Why are Gen Z using tech from a time they don't remember?

item.title: Alarm as Iran's 'sacrificing life' war campaign in Australia lets children sign up
item.description: The Australian Federal Police is aware of the campaign that has been promoted by the Iranian embassy in Canberra.

item.title: Israel–Lebanon ceasefire extended | SBS News in Easy English 24 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Markets slip as tech earnings loom and trust in finfluencers questioned
item.description: The ASX200 edged lower by 0.1 per cent, capping a weekly fall of 1.8 per cent, as global markets pulled back from record highs and investors turned their attention to upcoming tech earnings, with Tony Sycamore from IG Markets unpacking what’s ahead. Meanwhile, new data from ASIC reveals a growing number of young Australians are turning to AI platforms, social media and so-called finfluencers for financial information, raising concerns about credibility and oversight. ASIC Commissioner Alan Kirkland explains the regulator’s response.

item.title: The women facing 'impossible choices' as a crisis grips Australia
item.description: Cost of living pressures are thought to be exacerbating instances of domestic and family violence.

item.title: Government says 'no change' in position amid growing calls for 25 per cent gas export tax
item.description: Independent senator Allegra Spender said a 25 per cent tax on gas exports would help fix issues in the sector.

item.title: Amid war crimes investigations, some veterans are grappling with the Anzac legend
item.description: As Anzac commemorations are held across the country, many are reflecting on the legacy of service in the wake of alleged war crimes.

item.title: Fuel prices under threat, jobs transforming — and a new tax break for Australians
item.description: The key money stories this week, broken down simply and what they mean for you.

item.title: ‘Tea with heart’ – meet the military veteran giving back to others who serve
item.description: A chain of specialty tea bars founded by a military veteran is expanding rapidly. Ambi's Chai serves tea blended with exotic spices - and its founder is proud his cafes are bringing communities closer.

item.title: Fighting for a country that denied their rights: Remembering the service of Australia's Indigenous veterans
item.description: [[WARNING: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people should exercise caution when listening to this audio as it contains names and images of deceased persons, which may cause distress to members of these communities.]] Since the First World War, Indigenous men and women have served in Australia's defence forces, despite the government's refusal to recognise their rights. NITV Radio's Kerri-Lee Barry has spoken to Michael Bell about members of her own family who served in Australia's defence forces.

item.title: Meet the military veteran creating comfort and connection through chai
item.description: A veteran brews more than chai. He's stirring connection, culture and community one sip at a time.

item.title: Indigenous leaders, politicians and military chiefs condemn Anzac Day booing
item.description: Booing disrupted Welcome to Country addresses at Anzac Day dawn services in Melbourne, Perth and Sydney where a man was arrested.

item.title: Ultra-processed foods damage your focus and may increase dementia risk, study finds
item.description: Even small increases in ultra-processed foods can lead to poorer brain function, regardless of diet quality, a new study has found.

item.title: Sergio suspected his wife had been unfaithful. So he turned to 'love detectives'
item.description: In Peru, divorce is on the rise and infidelity can impact the division of assets. Private detectives like Liz play an important role.

item.title: 'Carrying things that aren't mine': Anxiety in the age of constant crisis
item.description: Constant exposure to global crises is fuelling anxiety and reshaping how people stay informed and when they step back.

item.title: Are migrant communities and non-citizen taxpayers being forgotten in cost of living conversations?
item.description: The war in the Middle East has impacted the cost of living in Australia, affecting communities across the country. Representatives for migrant and refugee communities are concerned needs are not adequately being addressed however, as economic and mental health impacts combine.

item.title: 'Talking about nothing': Peace talks in limbo as Trump cancels envoys' Pakistan trip
item.description: Trump said he called off the planned visit by his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner because the talks in Islamabad involved too much travel and expense.

item.title: Chernobyl, 40 years on: Survivors, silence and an exclusion zone
item.description: A rally in Sydney will mark four decades since the Chernobyl nuclear accident, amid warnings of ongoing nuclear risks in Ukraine.

item.title: Hanson says she is 'not against migration at all' as Taylor warns of threats from 'bad countries'
item.description: Pauline Hanson has addressed crowds at an anti-immigration rally in Canberra, just hours after Opposition leader Angus Taylor doubled down on his freshly-unveiled immigration policy.

item.title: Palestinian elections give some in Gaza first chance to vote in two decades
item.description: Palestinians voted in local elections, the first in two decades in Gaza and the first in the occupied West Bank since the start of the Israel-Hamas war.

item.title: Sandra will never own a home. Meet the boomers struggling in the housing crisis
item.description: While baby boomers still dominate property ownership in Australia, the rate has gone down. Some have been forced to find alternatives.

item.title: 'Serious consequences': Universities given fresh edict on Iran, Russia and North Korea
item.description: One university said its warning to staff came after it had received "updated advice" from the federal government.

item.title: Thirty years after Port Arthur, gun numbers in Australia are higher than ever
item.description: Thirty years after the Port Arthur massacre, rising gun numbers are testing Australia's gun laws and public safety approach.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Professor Margie Danchin on falling vaccination rates
item.description: A paediatrician says misinformation about childhood vaccines coming out of the United States has led to a huge spike in vaccine hesitancy in Australia in the past year. Professor Margie Danchin from the Murdoch Children's Research Institute says the latest figures show Australia has the lowest vaccine coverage for children under 5 in more than a decade, with the rates falling each year since 2020. She spoke with Greg Dyett.

item.title: Sabastian Sawe makes history by breaking two-hour marathon barrier in London
item.description: Kenya's Sabastian Sawe has become the first person to shatter the fabled two-hour barrier in the marathon with a breathtaking win in London.

item.title: Police investigate child abduction in Alice Springs | SBS News in Easy English 27 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: 40 years on from the Chernobyl disaster, leaders draw parallels with then and now
item.description: Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has met with his Moldovan counterpart in Kyiv, as the world marks the 40th anniversary of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster- and draws stark contrasts with the current state of play in Europe.

item.title: Gaza just held its first vote in 20 years. What did it reveal?
item.description: Gaza's first election in two decades saw low turnout in Deir al-Balah, as Fatah-backed candidates secured most seats.

item.title: Penny Wong warns IS-linked families face 'full force of the law' if they return to Australia
item.description: The foreign minister reiterated that her government will not assist their return as she prepares to tour Asia to shore up energy supplies.

item.title: Where did Australia rank as global military spending hit a record $4 trillion?
item.description: The United States, China and Russia accounted for more than half of global defence expenditure, new research reveals.

item.title: Iran foreign minister arrives in Russia as US talks stall, Israeli strikes continue
item.description: Abba Araghchi is expected to meet Russian President Vladimir Putin. Planned Iran-US talks in Pakistan collapsed at the weekend.

item.title: ASX hits three week low, as tax time scrutiny ramps up
item.description: The ASX slips to a three week low ahead of a pivotal week for global markets, with Raymond Chan from Morgans breaking down what to watch as central banks meet, US tech giants report earnings and fresh inflation data lands. At the same time, the countdown to tax time begins, with Assistant Commissioner Anita Challen outlining the ATO’s focus on work related deductions, side hustles and misleading advice circulating online.

item.title: How an Aussie 'battle taxi' invention spread around the world
item.description: Australia is investing $750 million to build more Bushmasters — but what makes them so desirable on the battleground?

item.title: How an Aussie 'battle taxi' invention spread around the world
item.description: Australia is investing $750 million to build more Bushmasters — but what makes them so desirable on the battleground?

item.title: Julia Gillard says 'manosphere' requires new approach to gender equality
item.description: Addressing reporters before the Women Deliver conference, she said "solidarity is not enough."

item.title: The secret behind the special grass for this year's FIFA World Cup
item.description: Here’s how researchers created the unique turf, which will give the same playing experience across three countries.

item.title: Investing for wartime: The online communities looking to make money from conflict
item.description: While many are feeling economic pain from war in the Middle East, others discuss how to make money.

item.title: Australians warned to brace for major inflation hit, as Trump's peace plans unclear
item.description: The US-Israeli war on Iran is threatening to destabilise the global economy.

item.title: Faith, social cohesion and dignity: The Pope's visit to Africa
item.description: Pope Leo has now returned to Rome after his four-nation African tour that bore witness to the continent's colonial past. The trip focused on faith, social cohesion and human dignity.

item.title: UK report raises concerns over AUKUS defence pact | SBS News in Easy English 28 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: 'Exploiting loopholes': New move could force tech giants to pay for Australian news content
item.description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said Australian journalism needed to be properly paid for by tech companies.

item.title: King Charles, Donald Trump and the test of a strained 'special relationship'
item.description: King Charles must stay above politics, but his US tour might be the UK government's attempt to play a "trump card".

item.title: Revealed: Here's what the ATO is cracking down on this tax time
item.description: Bad tax advice could come at a cost, the Australian Taxation Office has warned.

item.title: Diverging markets: why US and Australian shares are going in opposite directions
item.description: Australian shares extended their losing streak, with the ASX200 down 0.6% to a three-week low and ten of eleven sectors finishing in the red, even as US markets hit fresh records. Utilities led the decline, with Origin Energy tumbling, while a lift in oil prices and a rebound in lithium miners offered some support. For a closer look at the day’s moves and what’s driving the divergence with Wall Street, Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with MPC Markets CEO Mark Gardner.

item.title: Socceroo criticises Trump peace prize as a mockery of FIFA Human Rights Policy
item.description: FIFA's Human Rights Framework allows hosts to protect freedom of expression and prohibit discrimination during the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

item.title: Zelenskyy criticises Israel over grain shipments 'stolen' by Russia
item.description: Russia says that growing tensions between Ukraine and Israel are theirs to resolve.

item.title: Middle East peace proposal in deadlock as Trump, Iran clash over nuclear talks
item.description: US-Iran peace talks appear to have stalled again, with the US President reportedly unhappy with the latest proposal from Tehran.

item.title: Trump hails 'Two Kings' after meeting Charles, as the British monarch gets political
item.description: The US president has called King Charles "a fantastic person" though he has repeatedly criticised UK Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer.

item.title: Inflation nears three-year high: What could this mean for Australians?
item.description: The RBA is expected to raise interest rates for a third time this year following the release of the first economic data since the war began.

item.title: Fighting continues between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon | SBS News in Easy English 29 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: What in the Alexander Hamilton's name is the British king doing in America? SBS finds out
item.description: In a cordial and at times humorous address to the United States Congress, Britain's King Charles III has delivered a subtle pushback against the Trump administration. The high-stakes royal visit to Washington comes at a time of heightened tensions between the historic allies, with questions of shared values and security objectives casting doubt over the transatlantic relationship.

item.title: United Arab Emirates quits OPEC in a blow to the oil cartel
item.description: The United Arab Emirates has announced it will exit the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries - or OPEC - from May. Analysts say the decision reflects a growing rift with Saudi Arabia, and stronger ties with the United States. Any impact on oil prices is likely to be dampened by the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Australian Human Rights Commission warns sharp rise in racism may have violent fallout
item.description: The assessment also noted rising anti-immigrant sentiment, some scapegoated for cost-of-living issues.

item.title: A new country has become Australia's top source of migration
item.description: New Australian Bureau of Statistics data shows 32 per cent of the overall estimated population is now born overseas.

item.title: Does another news ban loom? What a potential tech levy could mean for you
item.description: Tech giants have rejected the government's proposed draft law, calling it a "government-mandated transfer of wealth" and "arbitrary".

item.title: Inflation surges as rate bets rise and markets continue to slide
item.description: A sharp lift in inflation and a seventh straight fall on the ASX set the tone for this episode of the SBS On the Money podcast, unpacking how global conflict is feeding into Australia’s economy. Official data shows annual inflation expanding to 4.6 per cent in March, driven by a surge in fuel and electricity costs. Harry Murphy Cruise from Oxford Economics breaks down what this means for interest rates and how much further prices could rise, while Damien Boey from Wilson Asset Management explains the market reaction, sector moves and what investors are watching next as US tech earnings come into focus.

item.title: 'Slap in the face to the regime': Exiled female footballers finally have reason to celebrate
item.description: After a five-year battle for FIFA recognition, refugee members of the Afghan women's football team can represent their team internationally.

item.title: 'We're not out of the woods': Australians told to brace for further fuel price pain
item.description: The NRMA's Peter Khoury says that further fuel price spikes are likely as an end to the war in the Middle East remains elusive.

item.title: This city has become a new investment hotspot, but the picture's not all positive
item.description: Investors' dream or renters' nightmare? The capital city seeing decade-high house prices, as buyers become more cautious.

item.title: Norway has tried it, and now potentially Canada. Could a public fund be a gas tax solve?
item.description: The gas export tax debate has reached a fever pitch — forcing the prime minister to weigh in on its prospects.

item.title: How much has the Iran war cost the US? Pentagon releases first official estimate
item.description: The figure provided is equal to the entire budget of NASA for this year.

item.title: New report calls for a nationwide strategy on AI in the workplace
item.description: A new report argues that Australia lacks a national strategy to regulate the spread of artificial intelligence in the workplace. It has been published as an A-I forum is held in Adelaide to examine that issue. The report from the John Curtin Research Centre argues that AI’s impact on Australian workers won’t be decided by technology, but by political choices.

item.title: Angus Taylor attempts to clarify 'bad country' remark as Iranian Australians respond
item.description: The Opposition leader has told SBS News that under the Iranian regime, the country "is not a safe, fair or free country".

item.title: 'Inevitable failures': Jewish groups respond to Bondi report, urge people to share experiences
item.description: The government will enact all 14 recommendations made by the interim report into antisemitism, following the Bondi terror attack.

item.title: Trump's stoush with Germany escalates further as he suggests reducing troops
item.description: The US president's Truth Social post came hours after Germany's top general met with US defence officials.

item.title: Costs of US-Iran war revealed as President Trump prepares to expand naval blockade
item.description: The United States is preparing to expand a naval blockade of Iran’s ports, as it ramps up economic pressure to force Tehran back to nuclear negotiations. US President Donald Trump says the strategy is working, but Iran has condemned the move as an act of war and signalled it will respond. Despite claims of military success, divisions are growing in Congress over the cost, strategy and long-term consequences of the war.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 30 April 2026
item.description: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Counterterrorism funds 'significantly declined' ahead of Bondi attack
item.description: The prime minister says the Bondi royal commission interim report shows "the opposite" and security funding had increased overall.

item.title: Chalmers has signalled a negative gearing shake-up ahead of the budget. Here's what might change
item.description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers said even if speculation is correct, "people shouldn’t expect there to be this huge amount of new revenue".

item.title: Family, community mourns as outback search for missing girl ends in tragedy
item.description: WARNING Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised that this story includes the name and image of an Indigenous person who has passed away. THIS STORY MAY BE DISTRESSING FOR SOME LISTENERS. The desperate search for a five-year-old missing in the Northern Territory has ended in tragedy. Police believe they've found the girl's body. Chief Minister Lia Finocchiaro says the entire Northern Territory is mourning the family's loss.

item.title: The first report into an antisemitism Royal Commission has been published. What does it say?
item.description: The Royal Commission on Antisemitism and Social Cohesion has tabled its interim report, just two months after the commission was launched. Lead commissioner Virginia Bell says the commission has not identified any security issues requiring urgent or immediate action. But the report makes fourteen initial recommendations, including the implementation of nationally consistent gun laws.

item.title: Oil breaches US$120, Big Tech’s AI returns
item.description: The S&P/ASX 200 has extended its losing streak to an eighth straight session, as oil surged past US$120 a barrel following comments from US President Donald Trump that he would extend the naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. The effect of rising oil prices is being reflected in company reports with Woolworths Group downgrading its full-year profit outlook, despite reporting a lift in third quarter food sales. For more, Stephanie Youssef spoke with TMS Private Wealth Portfolio Manager Ben Clark.

item.title: King Charles tickles Trump pink, Iran gets trolled - again and the bionic baggage guy
item.description: The special relationship is given the kiss of life, Iran’s new deal gets kicked into touch as Trump points to a long-term blockade and will Israel’s opposition bromance last? Plus, would you trust a robo-baggage guy?

item.title: 'Not one phone call': Families' plea for Australians detained by Israel, as DFAT assists in Crete
item.description: The Israel Defense Forces intercepted the Global Sumud Flotilla on Thursday near the Greek island of Crete.

item.title: 'Not one phone call': Families' plea for Australians detained by Israel, as DFAT assists in Crete
item.description: The Israel Defense Forces intercepted the Global Sumud Flotilla on Thursday near the Greek island of Crete.

item.title: UK terrorism threat level raised to 'severe' after London stabbing attack
item.description: The recent incidents come amid a rising number of antisemitic attacks worldwide.

item.title: The bleak reason this rental house in Adelaide is the only one of its kind
item.description: The single room is the nation's only rental that someone receiving JobSeeker payments can afford.

item.title: Andrie recorded a podcast on military influence — then came an acid attack
item.description: In Indonesia, an acid attack on a prominent activist has pushed concerns over military power and justice to centre stage.

item.title: Iran's supreme leader signals plans to maintain control of Strait of Hormuz
item.description: In a statement, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei suggested "legal rules and new management" of the strait will benefit the region.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese speaks to SBS about the antisemitism Royal Commission report
item.description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese speaks to SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson in the wake of the release of the interim report by the Royal Commission on Antisemitism and Social Cohesion.

item.title: 'A new historic chapter': First direct US-Venezuela flight in seven years touches down
item.description: "It's going to make life so much easier," a passenger boarding the return flight to Miami said.

item.title: Calls for calm in Alice Springs | SBS News In Easy English 1 May 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news bulletin for English learners and people with a disability.

item.title: Iran warns of "long and painful strikes" as US war deadline fast approaches
item.description: Iran is threatening "long and painful strikes" on US regional positions if the United States conducts any new attacks on their soil. As a deadline fast approaches for US President Donald Trump to make the case for Congress to extend the war, there is uncertainty around the future of a nearly month-long ceasefire. The crucial global shipping route, the Strait of Hormuz, remains an epicentre for the war's stalemate.

item.title: We all do it every day — but scientists still don’t know why. That may be changing
item.description: The findings of this study could provide an early clue into one of the body’s long-standing mysteries.

item.title: The fight for Farrer: How a rural by-election could signal a change in Australian politics.
item.description: The May 9 by-election in the New South Wales electorate of Farrer could have major implications for national politics. With One Nation and an independent candidate leading the race, voters in the formerly safe coalition seat represent a growing trend of dissatisfaction with the major parties.

item.title: ASX snaps losing streak as bank CEO says Australia will avoid recession despite property risks emerging
item.description: The ASX has broken an eight-day losing streak, lifted by mining stocks and easing oil prices, while global tech earnings continue to shape investor sentiment. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Morningstar’s Lochlan Halloway unpacks the day’s market action, as ANZ posts a higher profit and signals confidence Australia will avoid recession. Meanwhile, Coles flags further price pressures across supermarket shelves, and Cotality’s Tim Lawless warns Australia’s housing market may be entering a downturn as interest rates bite and growth slows.

item.title: Police charge man after stabbing attack in London
item.description: Police have charged a man with attempted murder in the stabbing of three people in London.

item.title: It was a 'sleepy' city — now it's one of Australia's hottest property spots
item.description: Saba moved to Wagga Wagga for a quieter life. Now she's witnessing a boom reshaping the city and its challenges.

item.title: Grocery price hikes, house values turn — and ATO’s tax time warning for Aussies
item.description: The key money stories this week, broken down simply and what they mean for you.

item.title: The world bought into these viral songs. Their similarities raised suspicions
item.description: Unofficial fan-made FIFA World Cup anthems are taking off on social media — but are they really what they seem?

item.title: Fires cost this couple their $1.8 million wine dream - and they’re not alone
item.description: As the Australian wine industry struggles with threats including natural disasters, one winemaking family is determined to rebuild after fires destroyed their entire first vintage.

item.title: INTERVIEW: SBS speaks to Travis Lovett on his 800km Walk for Truth
item.description: Kerrupmara Gunditjmara man Travis Lovett is now well into his 800 kilometre walk from Melbourne to Canberra. The former Yoorrook Justice Commissioner is leading the five-week long 'Walk for Truth' to call on Prime Minister Anthony Albanese to establish a National Truth Telling Process. SBS reporter Angelica Waite spoke with Mr Lovett when he was in Seymour, about 150 kilometres into his journey.

item.title: A $1.8m wine loss — and the growing crisis facing Australia's small producers
item.description: As natural disasters intensify, small producers are left navigating loss, limited support and an uncertain furture.

item.title: Budget under pressure | Midday News Bulletin 2nd May 2026
item.description: The government warns of mounting spending pressures ahead of the Federal Budget; Donald Trump says he will raise tariffs on EU automobiles; and in the AFL, the Crows triumph in an exciting finish against Port Adelaide.

item.title: Trump rejects Iran's latest proposal to end war
item.description: The US has said it did not want to exit ⁠its confrontation with ‌Iran early "and then have ‌the problem arise in three more years".

item.title: Six Australian Gaza aid flotilla activists released in Greece
item.description: Three released Australian activists have said they were subjected to violence and mistreated while on board an Israeli vessel.

item.title: Floral tributes in Alice Springs | Evening News Bulletin 2 May 2026
item.description: Floral tributes laid in Alice Springs for Kumanjayi Little Baby; fuel cost concerns rising for truck drivers; Jannik Sinner becomes the youngest man to reach the final of all nine Masters 1000 tournaments

item.title: A time for grieving, not making trouble: Kumanjayi Little Baby's grandfather calls for calm
item.description: Emotions are understandably running high, but it's not the time "to be a hero on social media or to make trouble", Robin Granites says.

item.title: NT woman charged following Alice Springs unrest | Morning News Bulletin 3 May 2026
item.description: NT woman charged after police car set alight during Alice Springs unrest; The US President rejects an Iranian proposal to reopen shipping through the Strait of Hormuz; and in AFL, another injury setback for St Kilda's Max King.

item.title: Government set to pull back budget spending as delayed economic shock threatens jobs
item.description: Economists have warned that Australia's historically strong employment rate could be at risk from global pressures.

item.title: Australia's student visa crackdown hits record highs — what it means for who gets in
item.description: Australia's student visa crackdown is reshaping who gets in — and exposing a system driven as much by politics as policy.

item.title: Colombians divided over the fate of hippos linked to Pablo Escobar
item.description: Colombia is confronting a unique problem: what to do with a population of hippopotamuses originally linked to Pablo Escobar. The notorious late drug lord became infamous for importing the creatures and establishing a zoo at one of his properties.

item.title: ‘Songs in Sign Language’ reimagines Disney musical moments in ASL
item.description: Disney Plus has released Disney Animation's Songs in Sign Language, which reimagines three animated musical sequences from recent feature films. The positive feedback to the project from the Deaf community in the United States has reached Australia, with advocates here hoping that it represents a positive step toward inclusion.

item.title: INTERVIEW: North Aboriginal Justice Agency CEO accuses NT government of long term detention in watchhouses
item.description: The Northern Territory justice system is under fresh scrutiny amid allegations people are being held in overcrowded cells for long periods of time without adequate healthcare in a Darwin correctional facility. A spokesperson for the Department of Corrections has said in a statement that the Darwin Transitional Custody Centre accommodates prisoners for a maximum of four weeks. They said prisoners undergo intake and medical screening to ensure they don't have any serious health concerns and are well suited to the environment. The spokesperson also said prisoners do have access to phones and can contact family. But this week, the North Australia Aboriginal Justice Agency (NAAJA) accused the government of failing to fully address the issue, saying parts of the Darwin City Watchhouse had been renamed and transferred to the control of the corrections department – while conditions continue to resemble a watchhouse. NAAJA CEO Ben Grimes is being interviewed here by Tee Mitchell.

item.title: NATO, top Republicans question move as US announces troop withdrawal from Germany
item.description: The German foreign minister said his country was "prepared" for a reduction in US troops and was discussing it with NATO bodies.

item.title: Kumanjayi Little Baby's family 'relieved' after charges laid, warns against politicisation
item.description: "Our little baby's passing must not become a politician issue or an argument," a family spokesperson has said.

item.title: Trump to review Iran's new proposal, warns of renewed US strike
item.description: The US president has said he "can't imagine" that Iran's latest proposal would be acceptable to him.

item.title: Man charged with murder of five-year-old girl in Alice Springs | Midday News Bulletin 3 May 2026
item.description: Man charged with murder of Kumanjayi Little Baby; Japan's Prime Minister urges Australia to deepen defence ties ahead of a meeting in Canberra; and in Formula One, McLaren's Lando Norris and Oscar Piastri take first and second place at the Miami Open.

item.title: 'Hard to swallow': Australia's Italian citizenship hopefuls dealt a blow by court
item.description: The ruling reinforces controversial laws enacted last year that limit eligibility through distant ancestry.

item.title: It's probably already in your fridge — and it could help in cutting risk of Alzheimer's
item.description: Eating seafood twice a week can be important to protect the brain from different diseases.

item.title: Suspected triple murder in Sydney | Evening News Bulletin 3 May 2026
item.description: Police investigate a triple-murder in Sydney; a man charged with the murder of a girl in Alice Springs; success for Australia at the World Athletics Relays

item.title: 'A worrying pattern': Man arrested after attack on nun in Israel
item.description: Christians in Israel experienced over 150 incidents of harassment and violence in 2025, according to one inter-religious organisation.

item.title: Leagh accidentally saw a photo on her partner's phone. It put her in an impossible position
item.description: These Australians found themselves caught in moral dilemmas. Here's what they decided.

item.title: The mental health 'safety gap' causing decade-long delays for vulnerable Australians
item.description: From referral to consultation, accessing mental health care presents many challenges for some Australians, a new report has found.

item.title: Bondi attack inquiry to hear of ‘everyday’ antisemitism | Morning News Bulletin 4 May 2026
item.description: Public hearings to begin in the Royal Commission on antisemitism; another ship attacked in the Strait of Hormuz as the US reviews another peace proposal; and in sport, Matildas captain Sam Kerr breaks a record for Chelsea.

item.title: Bondi attack inquiry to hear of ‘everyday’ antisemitism | Morning News Bulletin 4 May 2026
item.description: Public hearings to begin in the Royal Commission on antisemitism; another ship attacked in the Strait of Hormuz as the US reviews another peace proposal; and in sport, Matildas captain Sam Kerr breaks a record for Chelsea.

item.title: Suspected hantavirus outbreak on cruise ship kills three, WHO says
item.description: The World Health Organization said one of three people who died had a confirmed case of the virus.

item.title: Trump says US will start a project to 'guide' stranded ships out of Hormuz from Monday
item.description: It comes a day after Trump said he'd likely reject the latest Iranian proposal because "they have not paid a big enough price".

item.title: What is hantavirus, the suspected disease behind a deadly cruise ship outbreak?
item.description: Here's what we know about the suspected outbreak of a rare infection that killed three people on a cruise ship on the Atlantic Ocean.

item.title: 'Silent pay cut': The $24 billion super shortfall affecting Australians
item.description: A worker who is underpaid superannuation entitlements could have $30,000 less in retirement savings compared to a typical worker.

item.title: Budget won't be war between generations, says treasurer | Midday News Bulletin 4 May 2026
item.description: Treasurer says savings to outweigh spending and tax changes in the upcoming budget; Japan's Prime Minister in Canberra to meet Anthony Albanese; and in sport Manchester United legend Sir Alex Ferguson hospitalised.

item.title: Trump says US military to help stranded ships in Strait of Hormuz in 'humanitarian gesture'
item.description: The United States has announced it plans to guide ships stranded in the Strait of Hormuz. President Donald Trump has revealed few details about the plan he is calling Project Freedom, saying it is a humanitarian gesture. It comes as he considers a 14-point proposal to end the war put forward by Iran.

item.title: A tanker hit by projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz | SBS News in Easy English 4 May 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news bulletin for English learners and people with a disability.

item.title: Savings over spending: Chalmers says upcoming budget his 'most responsible yet'
item.description: Federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers has promised to deliver his most responsible budget yet with savings expected across every portfolio. Defending possible changes to negative gearing and capital gains taxes, the government says this budget is about adapting to the uncertain circumstances facing Australia's economy.

item.title: New agreement signed between Australia and Japan | Evening News Bulletin 4 May 2026
item.description: ** Australia and Japan strike a new agreement to deepen security and economic ties... ** A tanker hit by projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz as tensions escalate... And in sport, ** Daria Kasatkina wins first title as an Australian citizen.

item.title: 'Schools that look like prisons': Jewish Australians share stories in antisemitism inquiry
item.description: The mother of a young girl who witnessed the Bondi attack last year told the inquiry Jewish children are growing up in fear.

item.title: 'Hostage to volatility': Fuel prices tipped to spike again as excise relief runs out
item.description: Drivers are being warned to expect fuel prices at the bowser to rise in the coming weeks.

item.title: Albanese strikes deal with Japan on defence, minerals and fuel security
item.description: The so-called 'quasi-alliance' comes as China expands its military presence in the region and Australia's AUKUS submarine deal comes under scrutiny.

item.title: RBA expected to lift rates as Australian dollar nears four year high
item.description: Australian shares dropped 0.4 per cent ahead of an expected Reserve Bank rate rise, with losses across most sectors led by consumer staples and energy, while petrol prices continued to ease nationwide. Corporate headlines included a recall by A2 Milk in the United States, an ASIC investigation into Accent Group, and NAB’s weaker half-year profit driven by accounting changes despite solid lending growth and a steady dividend. The outlook for borrowers and interest rates is in focus as the Australian dollar lifts above 72 US cents. George Boubouras from K2 Asset Management Ltd joins the podcast to break down the market moves and what to watch next.

item.title: In pictures: Kumanjayi Little Baby’s family and community pay tribute to her
item.description: The family of Kumanjayi Little Baby have returned to where the five-year-old was last seen alive, as hundreds across the NT commemorate her death.

item.title: Australian Gaza aid flotilla activist returns home following Israeli detention
item.description: Two other non-Australian flotilla participants continue to be detained by Israeli authorities.

item.title: The RBA's rates call could 'come down to the wire'. This is what it means for you
item.description: If the RBA decides to increase the cash rate, it will be the third hike this year.

item.title: 'Dangerous escalation': US sinks Iranian ships, UAE fumes over missile strikes
item.description: The US attacks come after president Donald Trump's announcement of 'Project Freedom' on Sunday.

item.title: Three dead in boating tragedy off the far north coast of New South Wales | Morning News Bulletin 5 May 2026
item.description: Three dead in a boating tragedy off the far north coast of New South Wales... the United States shoots down multiple Iranian missiles... and Brisbane Lions player Koby Evans accepts responsibility for using a homophobic slur.

item.title: Ukraine and Russia declare separate ceasefires as enmity deepens
item.description: Political quarrelling has deepened between the two countries as the US' diplomatic eye has been drawn to the Middle East.

item.title: Electric vehicle tax break wound back, but it won't put the brakes on sales
item.description: Energy Minister Chris Bowen said changes to EV schemes would encourage car makers to focus on more affordable models.

item.title: SBS talks to Canstar’s Sally Tindall ahead of RBA rates decision
item.description: The Reserve Bank will make a decision this afternoon on interest rates, and financial markets and most economists are tipping a third straight rate hike. SBS' Cameron Carr spoke to Canstar’s head of data analytics Sally Tindall on Monday about what to expect. That includes the potential for a repeat of the its split decision to raise interest rates in March as the central bank frets over the dual threats of high inflation and a stalling economy.

item.title: Australians on board virus-hit cruise ship as passenger makes emotional plea
item.description: A US travel blogger onboard the stranded ship said there was "a lot of uncertainty" about what would happen next.

item.title: Tensions escalate after naval conflict in Strait of Hormuz, UAE oil fire
item.description: Iran says it has forced a US warship to turn back from entering the Strait of Hormuz, while the US has announced its forces have hit six Iranian small boats they say were threatening commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. They say US merchant ships made it through the strait - without saying when - while Iran has denied any such crossings had taken place

item.title: Heavy metal diplomacy: Australia and Japan strike deals on minerals, energy and defence
item.description: Japan and Australia have signed a sweeping package of agreements — sealed with an AC/DC drum skin and a very expensive melon.

item.title: US and Iran wrestle over Strait of Hormuz | Midday News Bulletin 5 May 2026
item.description: Rescuers among the dead after a yacht sinks in northern New South Wales... the federal government says a First Nations strategy is being developed to underpin reform to the NDIS... and Arsenal in the driver's seat to take the English Premier League title, after Manchester City slip up.

item.title: Three dead in a boating tragedy off the far north coast of New South Wales | SBS News in Easy English 5 May
Heavy metal and critical minerals: Australia, Japan sign deals on defence, economy and rare earths
item.description: Australia and Japan have signed a suite of deals strengthening economic and security cooperation as Japan's 'iron lady' makes her first visit to Australia as leader. With global energy disruptions and a faltering rules-based world order, both leaders say a strengthened alliance between Australia and Japan is crucial for both countries.

item.title: Borrowers hit again as the Reserve Bank raises interest rates | Evening News Bulletin 5 May 2026
item.description: Borrowers hit again as the Reserve Bank raises interest rates; The antisemitism inquiry hears Jewish families are living in fear; Stephanie Gilmore reclaims her Gold Coast crown in her return to surfing’s world tour.

item.title: FIFA World Cup preparations ramp up for the Socceroos
item.description: The Socceroos are ramping up their World Cup preparations, with just over a month until the 2026 tournament begins in North America. Coach Tony Popovic has a lot to do... and everyone has a lot to look forward to.

item.title: Award-winning author Craig Silvey pleads guilty to child exploitation crimes
item.description: Silvey was at one point one of Australia's most celebrated authors, behind popular books such as Jasper Jones and Rhubarb.

item.title: Does the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion go far enough?
item.description: The first two days of public hearings for the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion has heard from 24 witnesses who are Jewish Australians with lived experience of antisemitism. While there is broad community support for a Royal Commission, some argue it should be expanded to include other minority perspectives including Muslim and First Nations Australians' experiences of racism.

item.title: Seven cases of hantavirus now identified on cruise ship stranded off west Africa
item.description: Seven cases of the deadly hantavirus have now been identified in a suspected outbreak on a luxury cruise ship off West Africa. The outbreak has caused global concern, as the disease is considered rare among humans. What is happening with the ship, and what does hantavirus actually entail?

item.title: Experts warn Australians are underestimating flu risk as winter looms
item.description: More than 1,700 deaths were recorded last flu season, but experts say Australians still underestimate the risks.

item.title: Antisemitism hearing told man's public assault largely ignored on busy street
item.description: Nir Golan now covers his kippah with a baseball cap.

item.title: Interest rates rise again as markets slip and mortgage pressure builds
item.description: The Reserve Bank has lifted interest rates for the third time this year, pushing the cash rate to 4.35 per cent, even as other major economies pause. In this episode, PWC Chief Economist Amy Lomas unpacks what’s driving the decision and what it signals for the months ahead. The ASX edged lower, falling 0.2 per cent, with Todd Hoare from LGT Wealth Management breaking down how investors reacted. And with borrowers facing rising costs, Sally Tindall from Canstar explains what the latest rate hike means for mortgage repayments and how to assess whether your loan is still competitive.

item.title: Sina was forced to weigh a 'dreaded' decision — and fears the rate hike will spark another
item.description: The Reserve Bank of Australia has warned that the economic outlook is not good for Australians.

item.title: Tax breaks, super changes to kick in on 1 July as Chalmers flags more to come in budget
item.description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers has pointed to a number of other policies kicking in, amid muted cost of living budget expectations.

item.title: Trump pauses day-old Hormuz operation in latest push for Iran deal
item.description: The so-called "Project Freedom" was supposed to help vessels depart the Strait of Hormuz under US guard.

item.title: Trump pauses day-old Hormuz operation in latest push for Iran deal
item.description: The so-called "Project Freedom" was supposed to help vessels depart the Strait of Hormuz under US guard.

item.title: Iran ceasefire holding despite exchange of fire, US says | Morning News Bulletin 6 May 2026
item.description: The US says a fragile ceasefire is holding despite increased tensions over the Strait of Hormuz; the Reserve Bank explains why it's raised interest rates again; and in sport, Carlton fined over its handling of a mental health incident.

item.title: Suspected hantavirus patients to be evacuated from ship as contact tracing efforts sharpen
item.description: Dutch operator Oceanwide Expeditions has been scrambling to find a port that will accept the stricken ship, but claims a solution is in sight.

item.title: Teen radicalisation fears drive major online counter‑terror overhaul
item.description: Spies will monitor gaming platforms as authorities warn extremists are targeting young people through gaming platforms and chat groups.

item.title: IS-linked women expected to arrive in Australia within days
item.description: The federal government maintains it "has not and will not provide any assistance" to the group in their bid to return.

item.title: AFL to introduce new mental health rules after record fine to Carlton
item.description: Carlton have been fined $75,000 over their handling of an incident involving player Elijah Hollands in a game against Collingwood in April. The fine will be paid to the AFL's mental health partner, Headspace. The finding has highlighted the treatment of mental health within sporting circles.
